question_text: ECG stands for:
explanation: ECG stands for Electrocardiogram (also abbreviated as EKG from the German Elektrokardiogramm). It is a non-invasive diagnostic tool that records the electrical activity of the heart over a period of time using electrodes placed on the skin. It was first developed by Willem Einthoven, who won the Nobel Prize for this invention.

elimination_hints.hint: Echocardiogram uses ultrasound to visualize heart structure, not electrical activity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ECG with echocardiography

elimination_hints.hint: EEG (Electroencephalogram) records brain electrical activity, not heart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cardiac and brain electrical recordings

elimination_hints.hint: Electrocorticogram records electrical activity from the cerebral cortex surface, not the heart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cortical with cardiac recordings

question_text: The P wave in an ECG represents:
explanation: The P wave is the first deflection in a normal ECG and represents atrial depolarization (electrical activation of the atria). It occurs when the SA node fires and the electrical impulse spreads across both atria, causing them to contract. A normal P wave is upright, rounded, and lasts about 0.08-0.10 seconds.

elimination_hints.hint: Ventricular depolarization is represented by the QRS complex, not the P wave.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing P wave with QRS complex

elimination_hints.hint: Ventricular repolarization is represented by the T wave, not the P wave.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing P wave with T wave

elimination_hints.hint: Atrial repolarization occurs during the QRS complex and is masked by the much larger ventricular depolarization signal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that atrial repolarization is hidden in the QRS

question_text: The QRS complex in an ECG represents:
explanation: The QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization — the spread of electrical impulse through the ventricular myocardium via the bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibres. The Q wave is the first downward deflection, R is the first upward deflection, and S is the downward deflection following R. The QRS complex normally lasts 0.06-0.10 seconds.

elimination_hints.hint: Atrial depolarization is represented by the P wave, not the QRS complex.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing QRS with P wave

elimination_hints.hint: Atrial repolarization is masked by the QRS complex but is not what the QRS represents.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing what QRS masks with what it represents

elimination_hints.hint: Ventricular repolarization is represented by the T wave, which comes after the QRS complex.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing QRS with T wave

question_text: The T wave in an ECG represents:
explanation: The T wave represents ventricular repolarization — the recovery of the ventricular muscle after contraction. It is the wave that follows the QRS complex after the ST segment. The T wave is normally upright (same direction as the QRS) and represents the electrical recovery of the ventricles to their resting state before the next heartbeat.

elimination_hints.hint: Atrial depolarization is represented by the P wave.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing T wave with P wave

elimination_hints.hint: Ventricular depolarization is represented by the QRS complex.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing T wave with QRS complex

elimination_hints.hint: Atrial repolarization is hidden within the QRS complex and has no distinct wave on a standard ECG.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing atrial and ventricular repolarization

question_text: The PR interval on an ECG measures the time from:
explanation: The PR interval is measured from the beginning of the P wave to the beginning of the QRS complex. It represents the time taken for the electrical impulse to travel from the SA node through the atria and AV node to the ventricles. A normal PR interval is 0.12-0.20 seconds (3-5 small squares). A prolonged PR interval indicates AV block (delay in conduction through the AV node).

options.text: Start of atrial depolarization to start of ventricular depolarization
End of atrial depolarization to end of ventricular depolarization
Start of ventricular depolarization to start of ventricular repolarization
End of ventricular depolarization to end of ventricular repolarization

elimination_hints.hint: The PR interval starts at the beginning of P wave, not the end.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the start and end points of the PR interval

elimination_hints.hint: This describes the ST segment (from end of QRS to beginning of T wave), not the PR interval.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing PR interval with ST segment

elimination_hints.hint: This describes the QT interval, not the PR interval.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing PR interval with QT interval

question_text: A normal ECG has how many standard limb leads?
explanation: A standard 12-lead ECG has 3 standard (bipolar) limb leads (I, II, III), 3 augmented (unipolar) limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF), and 6 precordial (chest) leads (V1-V6). The 3 standard limb leads (Einthoven's leads) measure the potential difference between two limbs: Lead I (right arm to left arm), Lead II (right arm to left leg), Lead III (left arm to left leg).

elimination_hints.hint: There are 3 standard limb leads (I, II, III), not 2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Undercounting the standard leads

elimination_hints.hint: 6 is the total number of limb leads (3 standard + 3 augmented), but the question asks specifically about standard limb leads.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including augmented leads in the count

elimination_hints.hint: 12 is the total number of leads in a standard ECG (6 limb + 6 chest), not just the standard limb leads.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing total leads with standard limb leads

question_text: Which part of the conducting system is responsible for the delay between atrial and ventricular contraction as seen in the PR interval?
explanation: The AV (atrioventricular) node introduces a critical delay of about 0.1 second between atrial and ventricular depolarization. This delay allows the atria to complete their contraction and empty blood into the ventricles before ventricular contraction begins. The AV nodal delay accounts for most of the PR interval on the ECG. Without this delay, atria and ventricles would contract simultaneously, reducing cardiac output.

elimination_hints.hint: The SA node is the pacemaker that initiates the impulse but does not introduce the delay between atrial and ventricular contraction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing impulse initiation with conduction delay

elimination_hints.hint: The Bundle of His conducts the impulse rapidly from the AV node to the ventricles — it does not cause the delay.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing rapid conduction with delay

elimination_hints.hint: Purkinje fibres conduct impulses very rapidly through the ventricular walls — they speed up, not delay, conduction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing rapid ventricular conduction with AV delay

question_text: An elevated or depressed ST segment on an ECG is most commonly indicative of:
explanation: ST segment changes (elevation or depression) on an ECG are most commonly associated with myocardial ischemia or infarction (heart attack). ST elevation typically indicates acute myocardial infarction (STEMI) where there is complete coronary artery occlusion. ST depression suggests subendocardial ischemia or non-STEMI. The ST segment represents the period when the ventricles are depolarized and contracting.

elimination_hints.hint: Normal sinus rhythm has an isoelectric (flat) ST segment, not elevated or depressed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising ST changes as abnormal

elimination_hints.hint: Atrial fibrillation is characterised by absent P waves and irregularly irregular rhythm, not ST changes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing atrial rhythm abnormalities with ventricular ischemia signs

elimination_hints.hint: AV block is characterised by prolonged PR interval or dropped QRS complexes, not ST segment changes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing conduction blocks with ischemic changes

question_text: If the heart rate is 75 beats per minute, the duration of one cardiac cycle is:
explanation: The duration of one cardiac cycle = 60 seconds / heart rate (beats per minute). So, 60/75 = 0.8 seconds. During this 0.8 second cardiac cycle, atrial systole lasts about 0.1s, ventricular systole about 0.3s, and the complete cardiac diastole (relaxation) about 0.4s.

elimination_hints.hint: 0.4 seconds would mean a heart rate of 150 bpm (60/0.4), which is tachycardia.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect calculation of cardiac cycle duration

elimination_hints.hint: 0.6 seconds would correspond to a heart rate of 100 bpm (60/0.6), not 75 bpm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect division

elimination_hints.hint: 1.0 second corresponds to a heart rate of 60 bpm (60/1.0), not 75 bpm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing 60 bpm with 75 bpm

question_text: The pacemaker of the heart is the:
explanation: The SA (sinoatrial) node is the natural pacemaker of the heart. It is located in the right atrium near the opening of the superior vena cava. The SA node spontaneously generates electrical impulses at a rate of 70-75 per minute, which is the fastest intrinsic rate among all cardiac conductive tissues. This faster rate allows the SA node to override other potential pacemaker sites.

elimination_hints.hint: The AV node can act as a secondary pacemaker (40-60 bpm) if the SA node fails, but it is not the primary pacemaker.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing primary and secondary pacemakers

elimination_hints.hint: The Bundle of His is a conduction pathway, not the primary pacemaker. It has an intrinsic rate of only 20-40 bpm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing conduction system with pacemaker

elimination_hints.hint: Purkinje fibres are the terminal conduction fibres with the slowest intrinsic rate (15-20 bpm).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing terminal conductors with the pacemaker

question_text: In a condition where P waves are present but not always followed by QRS complexes, the most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: In AV block (specifically second-degree or third-degree), the AV node fails to conduct some or all atrial impulses to the ventricles. This results in P waves (atrial depolarization occurs normally) that are not always followed by QRS complexes (ventricular depolarization is blocked). In third-degree (complete) AV block, P waves and QRS complexes are completely independent of each other.

elimination_hints.hint: Atrial fibrillation is characterised by the ABSENCE of P waves (replaced by fibrillatory waves), not their presence without QRS.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing absent P waves with dissociated P waves

elimination_hints.hint: Ventricular tachycardia shows wide, bizarre QRS complexes at a rapid rate — P waves may be absent or retrograde.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ventricular rhythm problems with AV conduction problems

elimination_hints.hint: Bundle branch block widens the QRS complex but every P wave is still followed by a QRS. The AV conduction is intact.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing intraventricular conduction delay with AV block

question_text: Einthoven's triangle is formed by placing electrodes on which three points of the body?
explanation: Einthoven's triangle is an imaginary equilateral triangle formed by placing electrodes on the right arm (RA), left arm (LA), and left leg (LL). The three standard limb leads measure the potential difference between pairs of these points: Lead I (RA to LA), Lead II (RA to LL), and Lead III (LA to LL). According to Einthoven's law: Lead II = Lead I + Lead III.

options.text: Right arm, left arm, and left leg
Right arm, left arm, and right leg
Chest, right arm, and left arm

elimination_hints.hint: The right leg serves as the ground (earth) electrode in ECG and is NOT part of Einthoven's triangle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the ground electrode with an active lead

elimination_hints.hint: Four limb points are used in total for ECG, but Einthoven's triangle specifically uses only 3 points (RA, LA, LL).
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the ground electrode in the triangle

elimination_hints.hint: Chest electrodes are used for precordial leads (V1-V6), not for Einthoven's triangle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing limb leads with precordial leads

question_text:
Assertion (A): ECG is a valuable diagnostic tool for detecting cardiac abnormalities.
Reason (R): ECG records the electrical activity generated by the heart during each cardiac cycle.
explanation: The Assertion is correct — ECG is widely used to diagnose conditions like arrhythmias, myocardial infarction, heart blocks, chamber enlargement, and electrolyte imbalances. The Reason is also correct and explains the Assertion — since the ECG records the electrical activity of the heart, any abnormality in the heart's electrical conduction system or muscle is reflected as changes in the ECG waveform pattern.

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY ECG is diagnostic — it records electrical activity, and abnormalities in this activity indicate cardiac problems.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the causal link between recording electrical activity and diagnosis

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually correct — ECG does record electrical activity of the heart using surface electrodes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the basic principle of ECG

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — ECG is one of the most widely used cardiac diagnostic tools in medicine.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the diagnostic value of ECG

question_text:
Assertion (A): The SA node is called the pacemaker of the heart.
Reason (R): The SA node has the highest rate of spontaneous depolarization among all cardiac conductive tissues.
explanation: The Assertion is correct — the SA node is the natural pacemaker because it sets the rhythm for the entire heart. The Reason is also correct and explains the Assertion — the SA node generates impulses at 70-75 per minute, which is faster than the AV node (40-60/min), Bundle of His (20-40/min), or Purkinje fibres (15-20/min). Because the SA node depolarizes fastest, it overrides all other potential pacemaker sites (overdrive suppression).

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A — the SA node is the pacemaker BECAUSE it has the highest intrinsic firing rate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting firing rate with pacemaker function

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually correct — the SA node fires at 70-75 bpm, faster than any other part of the conduction system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the intrinsic rates of cardiac tissues

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — the SA node is universally recognised as the heart's natural pacemaker.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying a fundamental cardiac physiology fact

question_text:
Assertion (A): Atrial repolarization is not visible as a separate wave on a normal ECG.
Reason (R): The electrical signal of atrial repolarization is masked by the much larger QRS complex.
explanation: The Assertion is correct — there is no distinct wave for atrial repolarization (sometimes called the Ta wave) on a standard ECG. The Reason is also correct and explains the Assertion — atrial repolarization occurs simultaneously with ventricular depolarization (QRS complex), and since the ventricular muscle mass is much larger, its electrical signal (QRS) completely overshadows the small atrial repolarization signal.

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY atrial repolarization is invisible — the larger QRS signal masks it. This IS the explanation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding signal masking in ECG

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually correct — the QRS complex is generated by the much larger ventricular mass and temporally overlaps with atrial repolarization.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the temporal overlap of these events

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — no separate atrial repolarization wave is seen on a standard ECG.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking there is a visible atrial repolarization wave

question_text:
Match the following ECG waves/segments with the cardiac events they represent:
Column A: (A) P wave (B) QRS complex (C) T wave (D) PR interval
Column B: (1) Ventricular repolarization (2) AV conduction time (3) Atrial depolarization (4) Ventricular depolarization
explanation: P wave represents atrial depolarization (the spread of impulse across atria). QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization (impulse spreading through ventricles). T wave represents ventricular repolarization (recovery of ventricles). PR interval represents the AV conduction time (time for impulse to travel from SA node through AV node to ventricles).

elimination_hints.hint: QRS represents ventricular depolarization (4), not repolarization (1). T wave represents repolarization.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping QRS and T wave meanings

elimination_hints.hint: P wave represents atrial depolarization (3), not ventricular depolarization (4). QRS represents ventricular depolarization.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping P wave and QRS meanings

elimination_hints.hint: T wave represents ventricular repolarization (1), not AV conduction time (2). PR interval represents AV conduction time.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping T wave and PR interval meanings

question_text:
Match the following cardiac conduction system components with their intrinsic firing rates:
Column A: (A) SA node (B) AV node (C) Bundle of His (D) Purkinje fibres
Column B: (1) 15-20 beats/min (2) 70-75 beats/min (3) 40-60 beats/min (4) 20-40 beats/min
explanation: The SA node has the highest intrinsic rate at 70-75 beats/min (primary pacemaker). The AV node fires at 40-60 beats/min (secondary pacemaker). The Bundle of His fires at 20-40 beats/min. Purkinje fibres have the lowest rate at 15-20 beats/min. This hierarchy ensures the SA node normally controls heart rhythm through overdrive suppression.

elimination_hints.hint: AV node fires at 40-60 bpm (3), not 20-40 bpm (4). Bundle of His fires at 20-40 bpm (4).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping AV node and Bundle of His rates

elimination_hints.hint: SA node has the highest rate at 70-75 bpm (2), not 40-60 (3). SA node is THE pacemaker.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the SA node has the fastest intrinsic rate

elimination_hints.hint: Purkinje fibres have the slowest rate at 15-20 bpm (1), not 20-40 (4). Bundle of His fires at 20-40 bpm (4).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping Purkinje and Bundle of His rates

question_text: Statement: A standard ECG recording uses 12 leads to provide a comprehensive view of the heart's electrical activity.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. A standard clinical ECG uses 12 leads: 3 standard (bipolar) limb leads (I, II, III), 3 augmented (unipolar) limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF), and 6 precordial (chest) leads (V1 through V6). Together, these 12 leads provide a three-dimensional view of the heart's electrical activity from multiple angles.

elimination_hints.hint: The 12-lead ECG is the standard clinical recording. It uses 10 electrodes to derive 12 leads.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the number of electrodes (10) with the number of leads (12)

question_text: The time interval between the beginning of the P wave and the beginning of the QRS complex is called the _____ interval.
explanation: The PR interval (also written as P-R interval) is measured from the onset of the P wave to the onset of the QRS complex. It represents the total time taken for the electrical impulse to travel from the SA node through the atria, AV node, Bundle of His, and bundle branches to reach the ventricular myocardium. Normal PR interval is 0.12-0.20 seconds. Prolongation indicates first-degree AV block.

elimination_hints.hint: The QT interval measures from the start of the QRS complex to the end of the T wave (total ventricular electrical activity).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing PR with QT interval

elimination_hints.hint: The ST segment is between the end of the QRS (J point) and the beginning of the T wave, not between P and QRS.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ST segment with PR interval

elimination_hints.hint: The RR interval is the time between two consecutive R waves and represents one complete cardiac cycle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing RR interval with PR interval

question_text:
A 55-year-old patient is brought to the emergency room with chest pain. His ECG shows ST segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. The cardiologist suspects acute myocardial infarction.

Which wall of the heart is most likely affected?
explanation: Leads II, III, and aVF are the inferior leads — they view the inferior (diaphragmatic) wall of the heart, which is supplied by the right coronary artery (RCA) in most people. ST elevation in these leads indicates an acute inferior wall myocardial infarction (inferior STEMI). The left anterior descending artery supplies the anterior wall (seen in V1-V4), and the left circumflex supplies the lateral wall (seen in I, aVL, V5-V6).

elimination_hints.hint: Anterior wall MI shows ST elevation in precordial leads V1-V4, not in II, III, aVF.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing which leads correspond to which cardiac wall

elimination_hints.hint: Lateral wall MI shows ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5-V6, not II, III, aVF.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing lateral and inferior lead groups

elimination_hints.hint: Septal wall MI shows ST changes in leads V1-V2, not II, III, aVF.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing septal and inferior lead groups
